topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating constancy, mirroring the dull ache behind Sarah's eyes. She stared at the screen, the numbers stark and unforgiving. Her carefully constructed sense of professional equilibrium had just fractured. Liam, the junior analyst, had just presented his quarterly projections, and the accompanying compensation breakdown had been… illuminating. Sarah felt a sudden, cold tightening in her chest, a sensation like a vice gripping her ribs. She picked up her lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling heavy and insignificant in her trembling hands. Every word Liam had spoken about his salary seemed to land like tiny, sharp pebbles in her quiet workspace. She didn't look up, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the weak afternoon light, willing herself to simply fade into the beige cubicle wall.

[story 2]

Mark traced the rim of his water bottle, the condensation leaving a slick trail on his palm. The air in the open-plan office felt thick, suffocating. When the HR email landed in his inbox, detailing the salary disparity between him and young David, a strange numbness washed over him. He tried to focus on his spreadsheet, the columns blurring into meaningless shapes. A low, involuntary sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed too loud in the relative quiet of his corner. He kept re-reading the figures, the gap between his take-home pay and David's almost laughably small starting wage creating a persistent, hollow echo in his ears. He slowly pushed his chair back, the scrape of the wheels on the carpet sounding like a declaration of surrender.

[story 3]

Eleanor smoothed down her skirt for the tenth time, a nervous habit she hadn't realized she was engaging in. The silence in the breakroom was oppressive, amplifying the frantic drumming in her own ears. When she overheard the casual conversation about bonuses and performance metrics involving Ben, the realization hit her with a sickening lurch. Her jaw tightened, the muscles in her neck corded with tension. She picked at a loose thread on her sleeve, her gaze fixed on a chipped tile on the floor. She felt a distinct pressure building behind her sternum, a heavy weight that made drawing a full breath feel like an effort. She wanted nothing more than to retreat, to disappear into the anonymity of the hallway.
